I would have done the same thing if i was him.

guest_letters.title: Based website
guest_letters.body: Why aren’t we talking more about this IAC guy?? I just woke uop and checked bahut interesting site hai, there’s quotes and funny articles and board status?? We need more anons like iac to make cool shit. Im a very impressed pajeet. Writing this to see if it will get featured on the newspaper or not, im not sure how he set it up but if the !thing works then hello indus news! Im on da news niggaaaaaa
guest_letters.name: Narthie

articles.title: Weather for Indus today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of sacrilegious remarks moved over the board this morning, with Anon attacking sacred animals and dietary restrictions. The comments were marked by a cold snap of hostility towards traditional beliefs.

A scattered drizzle of comparative analysis fell later, as one anon sparked debate by comparing Sub-Saharan Africa to India. The discussion was marked by a front of racial tension.

A fog of social hierarchy rolled in during the afternoon, with discussions on caste, marriage, and identity creating a haze of complexity.

Meanwhile, a sprinkle of pop culture chatter touched on a potential gobarjeeta girl group and a light breeze of suggestion blew through about using random names instead of IDs.

articles.title: Anon debates sacred animals, diet, and faith
articles.body:
Ambedkar started the thread, saying having a sacred animal is "the most retarded thing a culture could do" and that eating beef is "based" and "just a fucking animal." Anon responded, arguing that the concept of a sacred animal is tied to a spiritual framework that not everyone shares.

Another anon noted that even if one doesn't care about animal welfare, the spiritual layer of a culture's beliefs should be respected. The discussion turned to the idea of establishing a new faith within the dharmic umbrella.

Anon said they would be indifferent to someone starting their own faith as long as they don't ask others to change. Anon also mentioned that Buddhism, for example, involves killing cows.

A respondent brought up the idea that dietary laws are tied to faith and not morality. Another anon disagreed, stating that choosing not to harm animals is a moral choice.

The conversation also touched on genetics and the idea of a "genetic bottleneck." Haggu claimed that Ashkenazis have exceptional qualities due to their genetic history, while another anon argued that muttification leads to outbreeding depression.

Anon proposed recreating the Indus Valley Diet with modifications, including a meat and seafood-heavy diet with millets, barley, and buffalo milk. Narthie later expressed frustration with the thread, saying it was ruined by a few users.

Nikhil Fuentes shared their personal experience of adopting a vegan diet and its positive effects on their mental health. The thread continued with discussions on faith, diet, and genetics, with various anons weighing in on the topics.

articles.title: Anon compares Sub-Saharan Africa to India, sparking debate
articles.body:
Soythie started the discussion by saying their country is experiencing fear due to an influx of people from Sub-Saharan Africa.

They described this group as having "0 impulse control like a homo erectus." Another anon, Kantabai Ownes, responded by mentioning that they learned about a Russian alliance with African countries from a documentary.

They claimed that leaders from Sub-Saharan Africa are often incompetent, corrupt, and make outrageous statements. Yui entered the conversation, making several provocative statements.

They expressed sexual fantasies involving Candace Owens and a Dalit girl, and later discussed Israel's actions in Uganda.

Nikhil Fuentes chimed in, stating that people from Sub-Saharan Africa are "not even human beings" and that one-third of their genome is not from Homo sapiens. They joked about Candace Owens and fantasized about having children with her.

Chuddie argued that preventing interracial mixing would help maintain a race's mean IQ, citing Brazil as an example. They claimed that people from Sub-Saharan Africa had 10,000 years to develop but failed to create a civilization.

The conversation continued with various anons discussing topics such as Israel's actions, the intelligence of people from Sub-Saharan Africa, and their own fantasies involving people from different racial groups.

articles.title: Caste, Marriage, and Identity
articles.body:
Mococo, a non-Brahmin of unspecified caste (UC), reported having common interests with a Kshatriya (KP) girl, but noted she showed less interest when he suggested activities like getting food or watching a movie together.

Lungi, a respondent, discussed how individual caste background and values influence marriage choices. According to Lungi, liberal upper-caste (UC) individuals tend to marry outside their caste, while traditional UC individuals tend to marry within their Varna.

Lungi cited examples of UC individuals marrying into lower castes (LC) or other faiths.

Narthie and Lungi also explored how mixed-heritage individuals might form their own subcultures over time, citing the example of Kayasthas, who originated from a mix of Savarna Shudra groups.

Lungi noted that Kayasthas exhibit significant variation in appearance and lineage. Some respondents, like Momojeet, expressed support for eugenics and selective breeding to create a perceived 'superior' caste.

Narthie pushed back against such ideas, citing personal experiences with loving and supportive mixed-heritage families. The conversation also touched on regional differences and social dynamics within castes.

Lungi noted that certain castes, like Jatts and Chamars, may refuse to intermarry despite being of the same Varna. Haggu added that Gujjars are often viewed as lower status in northwestern India.

Throughout the discussion, respondents emphasized the complexity of caste dynamics, marriage choices, and identity. While some advocated for strict adherence to traditional caste norms, others highlighted the diversity of individual experiences and preferences.

articles.title: Ambedkar discusses gobarjeeta girl group
articles.body:
Ambedkar started a thread asking if readers' sisters were auditioning for an upcoming gobarjeeta kumari girl group.

The OP expressed interest in seeing "pyaari behenas" from "ananmas" singing and dancing to "cringey but catchy zoomer songs." One respondent, Dyaush, expressed support for "NEpyari supremacy" and mentioned fond memories of a "momo wali pyaari /nep/yaari didi." Another poster, Lungi, described an imagined scenario of a "shitskinned poopjeeta" with "sunken dark eyes" and "dark knees" performing next to "chink angels." The thread included various reactions and jokes about the idea of a gobarjeeta girl group, with some posters expressing skepticism and others making lewd comments.

articles.title: Anon suggests random names instead of IDs
articles.body: Fuwawa proposed replacing IDs with random names for users. They suggested a list of names including Dyaush, Haggu, and Lungi. Momojeet and Aizen responded with suggestions and criticisms. Momojeet asked for more names and suggested adding Ambedkar. Aizen jokingly suggested adding Candace Owens and Nick Fuentes. Soyjeet panned the idea, calling it "dogshit" and suggesting a system with random first and last names.
